title: Global Surface Temperature Projections
description: Key temperature projections from AR6 Working Group 1: The Physical Science Basis. Global surface temperature will continue to increase until at least mid-century under all emissions scenarios considered.

data.report: AR6 WG1 - The Physical Science Basis

1.5C_crossing.note: All scenarios reach 1.5C in the near-term (2021-2040), best estimate early 2030s

title: Remaining Carbon Budget
description: AR6 WG1 assessed remaining carbon budget from the beginning of 2020 for limiting global warming to various levels with different likelihoods.

data.report: AR6 WG1 - The Physical Science Basis
data.note: At 2019 emission levels of ~43 GtCO2/yr, the 1.5C budget at 50% likelihood (~500 GtCO2) would be exhausted around 2032

title: Sea Level Rise Projections
description: AR6 WG1 projections for global mean sea level rise under different SSP scenarios, including contributions from ice sheets, glaciers, and thermal expansion.

data.report: AR6 WG1 - The Physical Science Basis
data.acceleration: Rate of rise has tripled compared to 1901-1971
data.iceSheetContribution: Ice sheet loss was the dominant driver 2006-2018
data.lowLikelihoodHighImpact: Under SSP5-8.5, sea level rise approaching 2m by 2100 cannot be ruled out due to deep uncertainty in ice sheet processes

title: Climate Impacts by Region - Middle East and North Africa
description: AR6 WG2 assessment of climate impacts, adaptation, and vulnerability for the Middle East and North Africa region, including extreme heat, water scarcity, and compound risks.

data.report: AR6 WG2 - Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability

extremeHeat.description: Severe increase in heat extremes; parts of MENA may become uninhabitable for outdoor workers
extremeHeat.labourProductivityLoss: Up to 10-15% reduction in outdoor labour capacity at 2C

waterScarcity.description: One of the most water-stressed regions globally; declining precipitation and increased evapotranspiration
waterScarcity.groundwaterDepletion: Critical in Arabian Peninsula, Jordan, Libya

foodSecurity.cropYieldDecline: 10-25% by 2050 at 2C warming for major crops
foodSecurity.importDependence: Many MENA countries import 50%+ of food calories

coastalRisks.description: Low-lying coastal areas and deltas face compound flooding and salinity intrusion
coastalRisks.atRisk: Nile Delta, Arabian Gulf coastlines, Red Sea coasts

GCC_specific.UAE: Extreme heat, sea level rise on coastal cities, coral bleaching
GCC_specific.SaudiArabia: Desertification, groundwater depletion, extreme heat events
GCC_specific.Qatar: Unbearable outdoor conditions during summer by 2050

title: Key Risks Across Sectors and Regions
description: AR6 WG2 identification of key risks from climate change including cascading and compound risks across ecosystems, food systems, infrastructure, health, and economies.

data.report: AR6 WG2 - Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability

keyRisks.detail: Biodiversity loss accelerates above 1.5C; 9-14% of species face very high risk at 1.5C, 12-29% at 2C
Coral reefs decline 70-90% at 1.5C, >99% at 2C warming
At 2C, an additional 800M-3B people exposed to increased water stress
Crop yield losses in low-latitude regions at 1.5C; fisheries decline
1B people in low-lying areas at risk by 2050
Cascading failures from compound events; $12-23T in global assets at risk
Poverty increase of 32-132 million by 2030 under high warming

data.adaptationLimits: Some adaptation limits already being reached: coral reef systems, low-lying islands, Arctic ecosystems
data.adaptationGap: Current adaptation is insufficient; gap widening especially in developing countries

title: Mitigation Pathways and Sector Decarbonization
description: AR6 WG3 assessed mitigation pathways to limit warming to 1.5C and 2C, including sector-specific decarbonization requirements and carbon price trajectories.

data.report: AR6 WG3 - Mitigation of Climate Change

sectorDecarbonization_for1.5C.electricity: Net-zero CO2 by 2050; coal phase-out by 2040; renewables 60-70% of generation by 2030
sectorDecarbonization_for1.5C.industry: -40% CO2 by 2030, net-zero achievable by 2050 with CCS and electrification
sectorDecarbonization_for1.5C.transport: -59% emissions by 2050; EVs dominant by 2035; aviation/shipping harder to abate
sectorDecarbonization_for1.5C.buildings: -42% emissions by 2030 via efficiency and electrification
sectorDecarbonization_for1.5C.AFOLU: Halt deforestation; agriculture emissions reduction 20-30% by 2050

title: AR6 Synthesis Report Headline Statements
description: The IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report (2023) distilled findings from all three working groups into headline statements on the state of climate change, future risks, and near-term action requirements.

headlineStatements: Human activities, principally through emissions of greenhouse gases, have unequivocally caused global warming, with global surface temperature reaching 1.1C above 1850-1900 in 2011-2020.

Widespread and rapid changes in the atmosphere, ocean, cryosphere and biosphere have occurred. Human-caused climate change is already affecting many weather and climate extremes in every region across the globe.
There is a rapidly closing window of opportunity to secure a liveable and sustainable future for all.
Deep, rapid and sustained reductions in greenhouse gas emissions would lead to a discernible slowdown in global warming within around two decades.
Climate change is a threat to human well-being and planetary health. Every increment of global warming will intensify multiple and concurrent hazards.
If global warming transiently exceeds 1.5C in the coming decades, then many human and natural systems will face additional severe risks.

urgencyMetrics.emissionsGap: Current policies put the world on track for 2.8C by 2100
urgencyMetrics.financeGap: Climate finance flows are 3-6x below levels needed by 2030

title: 1.5C vs 2C Warming - Difference Metrics
description: IPCC Special Report on Global Warming of 1.5C (2018) quantified the difference in impacts between 1.5C and 2C of global warming across key systems.

data.report: Special Report on Global Warming of 1.5C

extremeHeat.1.5C: 14% of world population exposed to severe heat at least once per 5 years
extremeHeat.2C: 37% of world population exposed to severe heat at least once per 5 years

waterStress.additionalPeopleAffected_at2C: Up to 50% more people affected vs 1.5C

cropYields.2C: Larger declines; maize, wheat, rice yields further reduced

species.1.5C_rangeReduction50%: 6% of insects, 8% of plants, 4% of vertebrates
species.2C_rangeReduction50%: 18% of insects, 16% of plants, 8% of vertebrates

overshootScenarios.description: Pathways that temporarily exceed 1.5C before returning to 1.5C or below by 2100
overshootScenarios.CDR_requirement: All 1.5C pathways with no or limited overshoot require CO2 removal of 100-1000 GtCO2 over the 21st century
overshootScenarios.risks: Overshoot increases risk of crossing tipping points and irreversible changes

title: IPCC Emission Factor Database - Key Categories
description: Summary of key emission factor categories from the IPCC Emission Factor Database (EFDB), used globally for GHG inventory reporting under UNFCCC guidelines.

data.guideline: 2006 IPCC Guidelines for National GHG Inventories (refined 2019)

industrialProcesses.note: Process emissions only; does not include energy-related emissions

title: Carbon Dioxide Removal Methods Assessment
description: AR6 WG3 assessment of CDR methods including costs, potentials, readiness, risks, and co-benefits for BECCS, DACCS, afforestation, enhanced weathering, biochar, and ocean-based methods.

title: Climate Tipping Points and Irreversible Changes
description: AR6 assessment of potential large-scale singular events (tipping points) including ice sheet collapse, AMOC slowdown, permafrost carbon release, and Amazon dieback.

tippingElements.consequence: Up to 7.4m global sea level rise
Up to 3.3m global sea level rise
Loss of carbon sink, massive biodiversity loss
Additional 14-175 GtC release by 2100 under high warming
Collapse of reef ecosystems, loss of fisheries and coastal protection

data.AR6_assessment: The likelihood of triggering tipping points increases progressively with global warming levels. Some elements may be triggered at 1.5C.

title: Fossil Fuel Phase-Out Requirements
description: AR6 WG3 assessment of fossil fuel infrastructure and required phase-out timelines to meet temperature targets, including stranded asset implications.

existingInfrastructure.note: If existing fossil fuel infrastructure operates as historically, committed CO2 emissions would exceed remaining 1.5C budget

phaseOutTimelines_1.5C.unabatedCoalPower: Phase-out by 2040 globally; by 2030 in OECD
phaseOutTimelines_1.5C.newFossilFuelInfrastructure: No new unabated coal; limited new oil and gas

strandedAssets.2C_pathway: 1-4 trillion USD in stranded fossil fuel assets

title: Global Grid Emission Factors by Country (2023)
description: IEA electricity grid emission factors (Scope 2) for the top 50 economies, representing lifecycle CO2 equivalent emissions per kWh of electricity consumed. Based on IEA 2023 data.

data.note: Values approximate and may vary by source methodology. IEA updates annually. Location-based approach.

title: Net Zero by 2050 Roadmap - Key Milestones
description: IEA Net Zero by 2050 Roadmap (updated 2023) setting out over 400 milestones across all sectors for a pathway to net-zero energy-related CO2 emissions by 2050.

data.report: Net Zero Roadmap: A Global Pathway to Keep the 1.5C Goal in Reach (2023 Update)

2025: No new approvals of unabated coal plants
Universal energy access (electricity and clean cooking)
60% of global car sales are electric (revised up)
2030: Annual clean energy investment reaches $4 trillion
EV share of car sales reaches 65%
Tripling global renewable capacity to 11,000 GW
Doubling the global rate of energy efficiency improvement
Methane emissions from fossil fuels cut 75%
2035: All new cars sold in advanced economies are zero-emission
Unabated coal completely phased out in advanced economies
2040: 50% of heavy truck sales are electric
Global electricity sector reaches net zero
Half of existing buildings retrofitted to zero-carbon-ready
All unabated coal plants phased out globally
2050: Net-zero CO2 emissions from energy sector
Hydrogen and hydrogen-based fuels: 10% of final energy

title: Global CO2 Emissions 2024
description: IEA data on global energy-related CO2 emissions for 2024, including breakdown by sector and fuel type, and year-on-year growth rates.

data.trend: Growth rate has slowed from pre-pandemic levels; clean energy deployment limiting what would otherwise be higher growth

title: Global Methane Tracker 2024
description: IEA Global Methane Tracker data on methane emissions from the energy sector, top sources, and abatement potential. Methane is responsible for ~30% of global warming to date.

reductionPotential.revenueGenerating_pct: About 40% of abatement can be achieved at no net cost (captured gas has value)
reductionPotential.IEA_target: 75% cut in fossil fuel methane by 2030 in NZE scenario

title: Global Renewable Energy Capacity 2024
description: IEA Renewables 2024 report data on global installed renewable capacity, additions, and growth rates by technology and region.

title: Middle East Energy Outlook - GCC Generation Mix and Renewable Targets
description: IEA analysis of GCC energy systems including current power generation mix dominated by hydrocarbons and announced renewable energy targets.

UAE.target: 44% clean energy by 2050 (UAE Net Zero 2050 Strategy)
keyProjects: Mohammed bin Rashid Solar Park (5 GW planned)

data.peakDemandDriver: Air conditioning (60-70% of peak demand in summer)

title: Global Energy Efficiency Progress and Targets
description: IEA tracking of global energy intensity improvements, efficiency investment, and the role of efficiency as the 'first fuel' in clean energy transitions.

data.note: Energy efficiency is the largest contributor to emissions reductions in the IEA NZE scenario through 2030

title: Global Hydrogen Review 2024
description: IEA data on global hydrogen production, demand, costs, and the role of low-emission hydrogen in the energy transition.

title: NGFS Climate Scenarios - Framework and Parameters
description: Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) Phase IV climate scenarios used by central banks and financial supervisors for climate risk assessment and stress testing.

netZero2050.description: Ambitious policies introduced immediately. Limits warming to 1.5C through stringent climate policies and innovation. Global net-zero CO2 by 2050.

below2C.description: Gradual strengthening of climate policies. Gives a 67% probability of limiting warming to below 2C.

divergentNetZero.description: Higher cost variant of Net Zero 2050 with greater policy divergence across sectors. More carbon removal needed.

delayedTransition.description: Annual emissions do not decrease until 2030. Strong policies needed after 2030 to limit warming to below 2C. Leads to higher transition risks.

NDCs.description: All countries achieve their Nationally Determined Contributions but no further strengthening. Warming exceeds 2C.

currentPolicies.description: Only currently implemented policies. Leads to high physical risks and over 3C warming.

title: NGFS Scenario Variables for Financial Stress Testing
description: Key macro-financial variables provided by NGFS scenarios for banking and insurance sector climate stress tests, including GDP, energy prices, and sectoral impacts.

creditRisk.description: Probability of default changes by sector under each scenario

marketRisk.description: Asset repricing under abrupt policy changes

realEstate.description: Physical and transition risk to property values

data.note: NGFS scenarios are exploratory, not forecasts. They are the standard framework for financial climate stress testing globally.

title: NGFS Sector-Level Transition Risk Assessment
description: NGFS assessment of transition risk by sector, categorizing industries by exposure to carbon pricing, stranded assets, demand shifts, and technology disruption.

title: NGFS Physical Risk Assessment Framework
description: NGFS framework for assessing physical climate risks including chronic and acute hazards, their financial transmission channels, and regional vulnerability.

chronic.temperatureRise: Gradual increase affecting labour productivity, agriculture, energy demand
chronic.seaLevelRise: Permanent inundation, saltwater intrusion, coastal erosion
chronic.precipitationChanges: Altered rainfall patterns affecting water availability and agriculture
chronic.oceanAcidification: Impacts on fisheries and marine ecosystems

acute.floods: River and coastal flooding; $50B+ annual losses
acute.droughts: Agricultural losses, water stress, energy supply disruption
acute.wildfires: Property destruction, air quality, ecosystem loss

title: NGFS Nature-Related Financial Risks
description: NGFS conceptual framework for nature-related financial risks, linking biodiversity loss and ecosystem degradation to financial stability. Published in September 2023.

data.report: NGFS Nature-related Financial Risks: a Conceptual Framework to guide Action by Central Banks and Supervisors

keyFindings.economicDependence: Over 50% of global GDP ($44 trillion) is moderately or highly dependent on nature
keyFindings.physicalRisks: Ecosystem degradation undermines economic productivity (pollination, water purification, soil fertility)
keyFindings.transitionRisks: New regulations (EU Deforestation Regulation, TNFD) create compliance costs

policyRecommendations: Integrate nature-related risks into financial supervision
Develop nature scenarios for stress testing

title: WRI Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas - Country Water Stress Scores
description: WRI Aqueduct 4.0 water stress scores by country, measuring the ratio of total water withdrawals to available renewable surface and groundwater. Scores 0-5 (5 = extremely high stress).

projections.by2040: 25% of countries projected to face extremely high water stress, up from 17% today

title: Climate Watch - Top 50 Country GHG Emissions
description: WRI Climate Watch historical emissions data for the top 50 emitting countries, including total GHG emissions and per-capita figures. Based on latest available data (2021).

title: WRI Aqueduct - Projected Water Stress Under Climate Change
description: WRI Aqueduct projections for how water stress will change under different climate and socioeconomic scenarios, highlighting hotspots of future water risk.

GCC_outlook.current: All GCC states already at extremely high baseline water stress (4.5+)
GCC_outlook.projection: Increasing demand from population growth and economic development compounds climate-driven scarcity
GCC_outlook.desalinationDependence: GCC produces ~50% of global desalinated water; energy-intensive and costly

title: GHG Protocol Scope 1, 2, and 3 Calculation Methodologies
description: Summary of the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard and Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Standard calculation methodologies for greenhouse gas accounting.

standards.corporateStandard: GHG Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard (Revised Edition, 2004/2015)
standards.scope3Standard: GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard (2011)

scope1.definition: Direct GHG emissions from sources owned or controlled by the reporting company
scope1.calculationApproach: Activity data x Emission factor = Emissions

scope2.definition: Indirect GHG emissions from purchased electricity, steam, heating, and cooling

approaches.locationBased: Uses average grid emission factor for the location of electricity consumption
approaches.marketBased: Uses supplier-specific, contractual, or residual mix emission factors

scope2.dualReporting: Companies should report using both approaches

scope3.definition: All other indirect emissions in the value chain (upstream and downstream)

upstream.name: Fuel- and energy-related activities not in Scope 1 or 2

scope3.mandatory: Required under GHG Protocol; increasingly mandated by CSRD, SEC, ISSB S2
scope3.typical_share: Scope 3 is often 70-90% of total corporate footprint

title: GHG Protocol Product Life Cycle Standard
description: Summary of the GHG Protocol Product Life Cycle Accounting and Reporting Standard for quantifying the GHG emissions of individual products across their full life cycle.

data.standard: GHG Protocol Product Life Cycle Accounting and Reporting Standard (2011)
data.scope: Cradle-to-grave GHG emissions of a product
keyRequirements: Define product, functional unit, and reference flow
Create a boundary that includes all attributable processes
Collect data for all life cycle stages
Allocate emissions using hierarchy: avoid > physical > economic

relationship.ISO14067: Aligned with ISO 14067 (Carbon footprint of products)

title: GHG Protocol - PCAF Global Standard for Financial Institutions
description: Partnership for Carbon Accounting Financials (PCAF) standard for measuring and disclosing financed emissions, built on GHG Protocol Scope 3 Category 15.

data.standard: PCAF Global GHG Accounting and Reporting Standard for the Financial Industry (2nd Edition, 2022)

assetClasses.attributionApproach: Pro-rata share of total project emissions

dataQualityScore.1: Audited or verified emissions from borrower/investee
dataQualityScore.3: Emissions estimated from physical activity data
dataQualityScore.4: Emissions estimated from economic activity data

data.adoption: Over 400 financial institutions globally have committed to PCAF
data.link_to_netZero: PCAF data feeds into Net Zero Banking Alliance (NZBA) target-setting

title: GHG Protocol Land Sector and Removals Guidance
description: GHG Protocol guidance on accounting for GHG emissions and removals from land use, land use change, bioenergy, and carbon dioxide removal (CDR) in corporate inventories.

data.standard: GHG Protocol Land Sector and Removals Guidance (Draft for public comment, 2023)

keyTopics.landUseEmissions: Emissions from deforestation, peatland drainage, land conversion
keyTopics.bioCarbon: Biogenic CO2 from biomass combustion and biological processes
keyTopics.removals: CO2 removals from afforestation, soil carbon sequestration, BECCS, DACCS
keyTopics.carbonCredits: Purchased offsets/credits should be reported separately, not deducted from inventory

accountingRequirements: Report land-related emissions and removals in the GHG inventory
Separately report biogenic vs fossil CO2
Track carbon stock changes over time
Do not net removals against emissions for Scope totals without separate disclosure

offsetGuidance.principle: Carbon credits/offsets must not substitute for value chain emission reductions
offsetGuidance.quality: Credits should meet high-integrity criteria (additionality, permanence, no leakage)

title: Global Temperature Anomaly Time Series 1880-2024
description: Global average surface temperature anomaly relative to 1951-1980 baseline, combining NASA GISS, NOAA, and HadCRUT5 datasets. The record shows unambiguous long-term warming.

data.note_2024: 2024 was the warmest year on record at approximately 1.29C above pre-industrial (1850-1900), first year to exceed 1.5C annual average above pre-industrial in some datasets

title: Updated Remaining Carbon Budget (2024)
description: Updated estimates of remaining carbon budget from 2024 onwards for 1.5C and 2C targets, accounting for emissions since 2020 AR6 baseline.

data.source: Global Carbon Budget 2024 (Global Carbon Project) + IPCC AR6 adjustments
uncertainties: Aerosol masking effect (reducing aerosols reveals additional warming)
data.note: These estimates have significant uncertainty ranges. The budget is highly sensitive to temperature target probability level.

title: UNEP Emissions Gap Report 2024
description: UNEP annual assessment of the gap between projected emissions under current policies and NDCs versus what is needed for 1.5C and 2C pathways.

data.keyMessage: The emissions gap is widening. Current NDCs are wholly insufficient to meet Paris Agreement goals. Immediate and unprecedented emission cuts needed.
data.G20_share: G20 members account for approximately 77% of global GHG emissions

title: SBTi Sector Decarbonization Approach - Required Reduction Rates
description: Science Based Targets initiative sector-specific decarbonization pathways specifying the emissions reduction rates required for companies to align with 1.5C.

data.methodology: SBTi uses sector-specific pathways based on IEA NZE and IPCC AR6 1.5C scenarios

powerGeneration.reductionRate: Scope 1+2 intensity must decline to ~0.04 tCO2/MWh by 2030

oilAndGas.scope3_requirement: Must include Scope 3 if >40% of total
oilAndGas.note: Updated FLAG guidance for land-intensive operations

transport_lightDutyVehicles.intensityTarget_2030: gCO2/km must decline in line with IEA NZE

cement.reductionRate_tCO2_per_tCite: Must decline to ~0.42 tCO2/t cementitious by 2030

steel.reductionRate_tCO2_per_tSteel: Must decline to ~1.2 tCO2/t crude steel by 2030

buildings_commercial.intensityTarget: kgCO2/m2 must decline in line with IEA NZE

financialInstitutions.requirement: Portfolio alignment targets using sectoral pathways

forestLandAgriculture_FLAG.requirement: Companies with >20% FLAG emissions must set FLAG targets

title: Stranded Assets Data by Fossil Fuel Type
description: Estimates of stranded asset risk by fossil fuel type under climate transition scenarios, based on Carbon Tracker Initiative and academic research.

naturalGas.LNG_terminals_at_risk: New LNG export capacity under construction may become stranded

keyRisks: Demand destruction from electrification and renewables
Investor divestment and rising cost of capital

GCC_exposure.risk: GCC economies highly exposed due to hydrocarbon dependence
GCC_exposure.mitigation: Diversification strategies (Vision 2030, UAE 2050) aimed at reducing exposure

title: Global Forest Watch - Deforestation Data 2023
description: WRI Global Forest Watch data on tropical primary forest loss in 2023, critical for corporate supply chain due diligence and FLAG target setting.

data.source: Global Forest Watch / University of Maryland
data.keyTrend: Indonesia has reduced deforestation significantly since 2016; Brazil saw decline in 2023 vs 2022; Bolivia and DRC trends worsening

title: Global Climate Finance Flows 2024
description: Tracking of global climate finance flows including public and private investment in mitigation and adaptation, and the gap relative to needs.

data.gap: Climate finance needs to more than triple to meet 2030 targets
data.NCQG_COP29: New Collective Quantified Goal of $300 billion per year by 2035 agreed at COP29, criticized as insufficient
